turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, the issue in this case is whether the confrontation clause of the Sixth Amendment or the Federal Rules of Evidence bar the admission of a witness ' s prior identification of his assailant on the ground that at the time of the trial the witness had suffered a loss of memory concerning the facts Now, the facts of this case are as follows. The case arose from a prosecution of the respondent Owens, who is a federal prisoner, for assault against a federal correctional officer, John Poster, at the Lompoc Penitentiary in California. The assault occurred on the morning of April 12th, 1982 , and it consisted of a series of blows to the head and arms of Foster that left him with verv severe head injuries. Foster was immediately taken to the hospital and spent about a Month in the hospital, during which time he suffered periods of qroqqiness and virtual incoherence, but nurinq some period of which he was relatively lucid and coherent, particularly towards the end of the FBI agent, who interviewerl him to determine as much as he could about the events of the assault. that time, according to both Foster's testimony at trial and according to the FBI agent ' s testimony , Foster qave, a · statement that described in great detail all the events l eading up to the assault and the facts of the assault , including identifying the respondent Owens as his assailant . Re identified OWens and then he also picked Owens out of a photo lineuo as his assailant. Now, at trial, by the time trial came around about a year and a half later, Foster had suffered a memory loss to some degree about the events of the assault . lie was able to remember much of the background of what had hapoened that day. He was able to remember some of the facts of what had happened when he-went into the TV room where he was assaulted. But the critical fact that he couldn ' t remember was the identit9 of his assailant. He could not remember in However, he coulrl rememher, and indeed he said he Mansfield, the FBI agent who had interviewerl him at the hospital. Re remembererl that statement, and in oarticular he remembered both that he had irlentifierl Owens as nis assailant at that time, that he had identifierl Owens from identification was correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he remember what owens looked like at the time he was testifying? But he remembered identifying him in the hospital, did he remember what the picture looked like that he identified as --

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: He remembered pickinq out that particular picture , yes. He was familiar --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but would he have been able to pick it out in the courtroom?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: He. would have been able to pick that picture out as Owens, but what he would not have been able to do in the courtroom was to pick that picture out as his assailant based on his memory at the time of trial. In other words -- QUF.STION: He zetetbered that he picked that picture out in the hospital?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Yes, that's correct, and he knew-that he picked out Owens, he knew what Owens looked like. What he was unable to say, and this is a mark in a sense of the precision of his testimony, was that he was able to sav, no, that I said it was OWens at the time, but he said, I honestly cannot tell you now that based on my current recollection that I have a picture in my mind of the assailant beinq Owens. He remembered a great deal about the assault, but what the picture was that he picked out . MR . BRYSON: That ' s right . That ' s riqht . QUESTION : l\nd he remembers that he -- at that time thought that that pciture was his assailant .

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Precisely, and he was confident that he was right at that time, but of course, hecause he has no is to say , the person who actually assaulted him at the time , because he has no recollection of that, he can't y say, yes, the person who assaulted me was Owens, to the extent he can say that the statement that I made I helieve<l at the time to be accurate and ! was confident --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr . Bryson , is that recollection that the man had sufficient to qualify him under the rule of evidence 602 that the witness not testify as to a matter unless the evidence is sufficient to show he has personal knowledqe of it? satisfied here for several reasonR, includin q the testimony that Foster qave on the stand. Of course, Rule 602 allows oersonal knowledqe to be estahlished hy means other than the testimony of the declarant , but I would have to point out of the case on that qround because of the harmless error rule .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the District Court never ruled on that did it? MR . BRYSON: Well, the District court did conclude that there was a basis for personal knowledge. The Court of Appeals disaqreed with that conclusion . - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. Okay, right .

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: -- on the qround that they felt thqt there was a disparity between the offer of proof that was made at the beqinning of the case and the actual proof that came in, but we feel that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do you think we need to do with regard to Rule 602?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Well, I don 't think it is necessary for this Court to rule on Rule 602. It would seem to if the Court reverses on the confrontation eiause and the Rule 80l(d) (1) (Cl issues that are before the Court, the Court is going to have to sen<l the case back in any event to the Court of Appeals, which can then rlecide whether a further remand is necessary. They may not think it necessary on the harmless error qround, but to the constitutional ques tion if you can qo off on some facing his assailant. seen the pipe. Also, there was an eye witness. I think it was inmate Jeffery who testified that the two were facing onP. another . Now, that is not a statement by the witness from the stand that, yes, I saw my assailant, but it is circum- was left up in the air by Court of Appeals . However, I availability for cross examination . Appeals disaqreed both on the confrontation issue the cross examination that was available in this case of under the rules insufficient, was insufficiently effective to satisfy the confrontation clausP, and it was insufficient to satisfy the r11le . Our view is that, turninq first to the confrontation clause , that memory loss on the subject of an out of court statement should not he a bar to the admission of that out clause are, the Court has said on numerous occasions, both to allow the witness to confront, ohysically confront against him in court, and, of course , oart and oarcel of that is , have an opportunity for cross examination of the witnesses . Now, the fact of memory loss does not in our view interfere with those basic principles of the confrontation clause . Memory loss is a fact of life in criminAl anci civil cases . There is typically some memory loss in virtually every case, particularly if a comes on for trial a year, a year and a half after the fact, even if there has been no trauma, as there was in this case , and even if there is no inte11tional effort to r\issemhle on the oart of the witness, as there has been in some other Courts of a witness . is apt· to have made either a mistake or be now quilty of a Now, in our view , based on these principles, we would say that the confrontation clause is satisfied if, Number One, the scope of the cross examination is not or rule , This is a ptinoiple that comes from this Court 's Number Two, if the witness is competent, that is say, if the witness is able to enqage in a question and answer dialogue, if the witness is mentally and physically able to engage in cross examination. And Number Three, if the witness does in fact engage in this cross exaMination process by answering questions as opposed to simply refusing to answer questions altoqether. Now, the respondent contends that in this case worse than it is when you are talking about a witness who is t estifying about matters that he observed that he is testifying in court but ha s suffered loss of memory with respect to those matters.. He concedes for the most part, I think, that there is no violation of the confrontation clause if , as in a case such a s t h is Co u rt ' s deci sion in Delaware a g a inst Fensterer, the witness simply has a shaky memory as to some events as to which he is testifying . Even if there is a substantial loss of memory on the part of the witness so that the witness as, again, in Delaware aqainst Fensterer , says, well, I know what my opinion is but I can ' t remember any of the reasons why I reached that opinion, because he is testifying about his current opinion in Court, the says, that is different from this case, where the witness is not testifying about his in court observations , or is testifying about h i s observations , whil e he is standi ng there is present recollection of his observations while he is in court, but is testifying about an out of court statement. In our view, there is no such clearcut line to be drawn between an out of court statement and in court testimony about one ' s current recollection, and I think the example that perhaps can best make this ooint

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask, Rryson, didn't Fensterer as screened leave open this question?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: It certainly did, Your Honor . This open, we think that the analysis of Fensterer is helpful in should reach. with me as I say that. have forgotten a l o t about what happened. '!'wo different to myself and thinkinq, that ' s Jones , my recollection is now, in court testimony of the Fensterer sort . A second thing that could happen is, I could say, well , I remember saying to my associate, that ' s Jones coming out of the hank . Now , if r have forgotten the basis, in large part or in whole, of either of those two statements, either the one I made to myse lf, in effect, or the one r · made to my associate, if I have forgotten why it was that I made those statements, Fensterer would still allow the admission of the first statement . We submit that there is no real difference between that and the admission of the second statement, that the degree of the effecti veness of cross examination, the way the cross examination would ao would not be in any sianificant degree different in those two cases . We don't see a reason to draw a line between those two cases . Now, the case is harder, of course, if in fact the witness himself has no recollection of the prior state- ments other than the prior statement has to come in through a third party. But even in that case, which is not this case , in this case there was a prior recollection hy the witness of the statement , even in that case there is fruitful line of cross examination that can be enoaged in. For example, the could be probed for bias . The witness ' s general credibilitv can be probed under cross available for cross examination, even if my memory is not good, as long as I am available for cross exa111ination, it to the out of court statement. Now, there is some disagreement , to be sure, among the Courts of Appeals as to exactly how much recollection I have to have concerning the statement in order for the statement of the third oarty to be introduced, but I don't

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this case did the FBI agent testify?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: He did, but he testified merelv to corroborate the fact that the statement was made. testimony of Foster was, as to thP contents of the statement was as complete as anything the FBI agent QUESTION : Well , Mr. Rryson, I aather if nwens had died after the FBI interview and before the trial, the aqent could not have testified. MR . BRYSON: I think that is probably riqht, at least not under 80l(d) (l) (Cl . Probably no -- QUESTIOtl: Well, in this case didn't owens finally die?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, OWens --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Didn't he? MR . BRYSON: OWens' memory died in part in this case in only a small, admittedly critical but nonetheless narrow part of the recollection of the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, to the extent admittedly critical , then why is this any differept than had OWens died?

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Because there is so much more that can be done with Foster on the stand, available for cross examination, than if Foster were not there . You can probe such matters as his bias. Suppose, for example -- it wasn ' t true in this case, but suppose he had been trying to get Owens for months, and Owens knew and he passed this on to his attorney, that this was -- that his attorney could try to make use of that fact to impeach Foster ' s testimonv on the grounds of bias, something that would have been much harder to do if Foster were not present . Similarly , the question of Foster's credibilitv can be probed, whether his demeanor suaqested that he was a careful person or a person who was quite careless, the take this case , which does come up every day , the bank teller has a robber come in , put a qun in his or her face , and the bank teller qives over the money , a nd then later that afternoon a suspect is captured . The bank teller qoes down to the po'. ice station , and there is an In . The bank teller selects , without any doubt , selects 3 as the person who put the qun in her face . A year and a half later , tria l comes on and the bank te l ler cannot make an identification in court , just can ' t pick the defendant out . was devised , cases where memory at trial has in your wor.is died . There is no reco l lection as to iden ti f i ca tion Certainly it is a critical fact . It is the most critical fact in the case . we submit that the rule is satisfied , and we submit . that because the bank teller is available for cross e xami nation , particularly if she can remember mak inq the statement and the deta i ls of the stat ement , that in that case the confrontation clause is satisfied as well. QUF.STION : Mr . !lryson , if Rule 80 4 savs a witness is unavai l able when he does n ' t recall the substance 0f hi s prior statements , then how can the same witness be subiect to that, and it, and it is interesting and to us probative that in fact Congress in eections that were so close together used very different language . He clearly was in this case by anv construction of the terms subject to cross examination about or concerning the statement . He remembered the statement. Fe was subject to cross examination . Re was cross examined about the statement. The only thing he was not subject -- well, he was subject to cross examination, but the only thin g he could not fruitfully be c r oss exarninerl on was the underlying basis for the statement, to wit: the - - how it was that he observed Owens. That in our view does not make Rule 80l(rl) (1) (C) --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he could also be asked whether or not he remembers the defenrlant who is sitting there in the courtroom, does he remember him as the o ne who

turns.name: ARYSON
turns.text: He could he asked that, and he was, and he denied it . He sairl, I don't remember now the defendant; however, what be did say was

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He is the same as in the picture. is the one I picked . out, he is the one I identified, and of course another line of cross examination could have been fruitful on this score, but wasn't because of the circum- stances of the case . Suppose Foster had not known <>wens for very long, Owens had just arrived in the cell area . The question whether he would recoqnize Owens could be raised, but in fact, of course, because he knew Owens very well, it was difficult to make that point on cross examination . Again, cross examination was not rendered ineffectiv for constitutional purposes simply because it didn't work . Now, as I have indicated, the effectiveness test that respondent ha!! snqgested is one that h as a lot of problems . To try to make the confrontation clause turn on the extent to which confrontation of cross examination may be effective in a particular case runs into what this r.ourt said in Roberts is an inevitably nebulous threshold of effectivenes Respondent has offered no real standard for determining in any class of cases when cross examination is effective and when it is not effective. And in fact, as we have suqqested, memorv loss may not render cross examination less effective. It may render it more effective. It just depends on the particular circumstances of the case, and what ,really has more effect on effectiveness than simple fact memory loss is what to say that the constitutional principle of confrontation a particular case would, we submit, be chaotic as an aoproach to try to give the lower courts and the state courts in applying·the confrontation clause any quidance at all as to how the admissibility of statements should be judqed . That is a principle that ouqht to be reserved for the rules of evidence as this Court, and the states work them out. It should not be a constitutionally bindinq principle for all time that turns on a court ' s impression as to how effective the opportunity for cross examination was on a particular fact situation . And I would point out finally that the -- to the extent that effectiveness is a question , as I have suqqested in this case, the cross examination was effective. There were things that were brouqht out about prior statements Foster had made in the hospital, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What it says under the rule is that the cross examination contemplated must be sufficient to provide the jury with an adequate basis for assessinq the reliability and truthfulness of the statement. Is that satisfied here? that's -- we think that the lanquage• of the rule doesn't require that the jury be satisfied to any de qree. All that the language of the rule requires is that the declarant be subject to cross examination concerning the statement. Even if he has a virtually total lack of recollection as to the statement , that is a fact that the jury can assess in det e rmini ng his credibility o r determining the likely reliability of the stateme nt . We don't think that he has to have any soecific degree of recollection with respect to the contents of the statement , or certainly not with respect to the basis of the statement , that is, why he reacned the conclusion that he did. Thank you .

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr . Bryson. We will hear now from you, Mr. Ides.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: Mr . Chief Justice, and may it olease the Court, I would like to irrtmediately address some of the points that were raised during Mr. Bryson's arqUrrtent . First, Justice O' Connor , on the question of personal knowledae, the government is mistaken. The Oistrict Court made Absolutely no finding on the question of personal knowledqe. Oistrict Court stat.ed that personal knowledqe merely qoes to the Col.rt or lhat i We aqree completely that if ,'lt all Possible, this case ought to be resolved on the statutory questions, and in fact before the Ninth Circuit our arqument was based largely on Rule 602 and Rule 80l(d) (1) (C). As to the evidence of personal knowledge that th.:! qoverl\l'lent for first time brings up today·. --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you arque that as an alternate basis for affirmance in your brief here, Mr. Ides, the 601 ooint? MR . IDES: 602 point?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: 602 point.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: The alternate basis for affirmance we have relied on is 80l(d) (1) (C), although we have mentioned the 602 point in the brief and discussed it .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you haven't really relied on it as an alternate· basis for affirmance? MR. We .did not because the Court of Appeals didn't resolve that issue . I would like to just mention a couple of points on the factual statements the qovernment made, the fact that it was a pipe, that he saw that it was a pipe somehow indicates that he saw what was qoinq on. Well, the testimony was that "I heard something hit my head and it sounded like a pipe," that doesn ' t indicate that he saw anybody. His In the eyewitness testimony that someone saw him facinq, Foster facing his assa ilant, that is not what the testimony was. The testimony was that the 11ss11ilant was hoverinq over Foster . We do n't know which direction 'Ir. Foster • s face was pointing in . Of course, no findings have on these points . other point that I think is very import11nt, Mr. Justice White, you mentioned the ohotoqraph, and the photoqraphic spread that Foster recalls havinq identified Owens in . It is irrelevant, and it is irrelevant for this reason . Foster knew OWens intimately . They worked toqether every day . When he identified him in the photo spread , he merely said, yes, that 's Owens. So the fact that he could say, yes , Owens struck me , and that's a picture of this person who I have known for the past year or so doesn ' t indicate a circumstance where he has made an identification of an anonymous oerson 11nd then in court says, yes , that ' s the person I identified. tt is merely affirminq the fact that he knows Owens, so the photographic spread really adds no weiqht to the qovernment's case whatsoever . At issue in this case is a riqht of trial nrocedure that goes to the inteqrity of the factfindinq orocess . are not talking about an exclusionary rule . Ne are not designed to protect or to allow Police an adequate of discretion in the field . Ne are talking about " very of the trial and the ultimate inteqrity of the factfindinq process . This Court has stated on nu:-1erous occasions, the Mos recently Kentucky v . Stincer, that the confrontation clause and the right of cross examination are functional riqhts designed to protect the inteqrity of the factfindinq process and to create a basis upon which the reliability of testi- menial can be assessed by the trier of fact . The question in this case is not whether a memor y loss nrecludes confrontation . question is what is the iMpact on cross the government concedes , an assertion of the Fifth Amendr.lent a refusal to testify on other qrounds , incapacity of the witness, or some trial court restriction on the scope of cross examination. In all this Court ' s cases the focus is not on the particular intrusion, but on the impact of that intrusion, and that is what the issue is in this case . According to the qovernment , the confrontation clause is satisfied essentially by presence At trial, a physical presence model , and I think it is fair to say that there is neither judicial nor scholarly supnort for who, as I will discuss, if I can get to it, has a very similar model for reliability under the due process clause . The government is really staking out radically new qrounds for this Court, and it is an illoqical qround. For example, the government tells us that clearly if a witness refuses to tesify baserl on the Fifth Amendrrlent, as in Douglas versus Alabama , that would violate the confron- tation clause. Clearly, if a court, as in Davis versus the confrontation clause. our case? Suppose, for example, instead of saying I don't remember, Mr. Foster said, I take the Fifth Amendment. Or suppose Mr. Foster said, or suppose the Court said, I am not be because the defendant's ability to assess the reliability of the testimony and to provide a basis for case, and it is a narrow case, it is a small case, if there is a total memory loss, and in this case a medically certifiab e memory loss . We are not talking about circumstances which it is a partial memory loss or which the witness can ' t remember decide this case the way you want us to. I think this opinion can be written very narrowly, and I only the most extraordinary case where there has been Alaska. in that case. The typical case of the bank teller, though, is the circumstance where, when it comes ' thre e ye ars · later and you come to trial, the bank teller can say, I was working at the bank, a person walked up to me, put a qun in my face . I looked at that person, they told me to turn over the money, I turned over the money, on that day the was well lit, it took about 35 or 40 seconds, to tell you the truth, I can ' t remember exactly what the person looked. like today , though one week after that I did identify that person in a tbat is very different. We now have an opportunity to cross examine that bank teller as to the basis for the identification. He or she may not remember now who the exact person was, but they are available to be tested as to the basis for makinq that identification, and that is the key to identification evidence. And the qovernment has pointed out, and Justice Brennan has pointed out,it was the critical evidence in this case. remembers is that she saw him well enouqh to identify him and did identify him at a later time . would that come within your rule here or not?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: No, I don't think it would, and the reason it wouldn't is because we have now established a basis for the trier of fact to sit down and look at what the actually saw .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No , she doesn ' t remember. She doesn ' t remember whether it was bright or dim . She hasn't qiven any information on these·suojects. What I am sayinq is that the Chief Justice's concern is a real one . How much does the teller have to remember about the actual identification day in order not to fall within the rule you are espousing?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: She has to remember at least this, that she saw the person and had an opportunity to observe the person . That is critical , I think Louisell and Mueller point out in their text that in BOl(d) (ll as with the clause there is a critical relationship between personal knowledqe and the identification. But how does that make cross examination see him profile or head on? Was he close or far? r clon • t remember any of that. All I remember is that I saw

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: But she did say that I remember that I he had ever seen his assailant. lie never testified that I saw him but I now can ' t remember what he looked like. What he testified to was, I don ' t remember if I ever saw him .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that that distinguishes this case . What I am asking is, why that should make a difference between one teller who remembers no more than that and the next one who remembers one aclditional factor .

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: Wel:i., again, that is qoing to <lepend on a case by case analysis. There is no question about this . But it seems to me what we are qoing to have to look at is first, is the loss of memory directed toward a critical part of the case; second , is the loss of memory such that you cannot assess the credibility of the person based on And in this case, as to the only critical evidence that Foster could give, it was complete . There was no way to cross examine him on that point. No way to establish his personal knowledqe or lack of personal knowledge. No way to establish his opportunity to observe .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask, confining it to the a dying declaration, and they tried to put in the through you distinguish the case?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: I do think it would violate the confron- tation clause for exactly the same reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So all dying declarations violate the confrontation clause?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: Under those circumstances it to me they would unless you could establish that there were independent indicia of reliability, and it may be, in fact, this Court has held that a dying declaration is firmly rooted in our jurisprudence, and therefore uneer those circumstances it would be reliable . This exception to the hearsay rule is not firmly routed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am hypothesizing a dying declaration where you have no reason to believe there was any motive to lie, which I think is this case. I don ' t think there is any claim that this man was not credible to the extent -- !IR . IDES: That is true .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Rut you do have total absence of ability to find out why the declarant concluded that the person he or she identified was the assailant.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: Well, I think the first arqument in that case would be personal knowledqe, and aqain we come back to that because it is really -- it is the essence of this evidence. We come back to that because it is really the essence of ·· tnis evidence, if Foster doesn 't have personal knowledge of who his assailant was, then the evidP.nce shouldn't be admissible, and we have that problem in that same circumstance. Because of the nature of what Foster told Mansfield, he didn ' t tell Mansfield the details of the crime . The question was, who did it, Owens did it . I jammed my finger into the chest of the person who assaulted me. Period . That ' s all we got . So it seems to me it would present the same problems of confrontation. Let ' s assume, in fact, Foster a dying declaration, but then he may have died six months later or a year later. Assume he hadn ' t testified, and the only evidence we had was Mansf iP.ld on the stand saying he made that statement to me. There is no way that that would satisfy the confrontatio clause under this court's rulinqs, and in essence the identifying this person , and it seems tha t even the qovern - evidence it would violate the confrontation clause . The cases are on the same footinq .

I QUESTION : Well , he not onlv lookeci at the photograph and says, that ' s the man who assaulted me, but his name is Owens . MR . IDF.:S: Well , he rlid it in reverse order . He said, Owens , who I personally know , assaulted me, and here ' s his picture .

He couldn ' t remember those visits, and it is quite possible that one of them said, Owens is under investiqation . He is the guy who hit you.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES

turns.text: Well, suppose after he said this is Joe Dokes, he wrote it down in a note to his brother. Would that help him? MR . IDES : The fact that he just wrote it down is Just merely another out of court identification. Again, the crucial evidence in this case is, I saw the person who struck me, and that is what we want to explore in this case. It is locked in a little box which you never know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do you do that?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why couldn ' t you have asked the question without anybody saying anythinq? MR. !DPS: Asked time of trial seeing his assailant. His answer wa,;, no, I can't remember.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, what is wronq with that? the best way to answer that is contrasting this case with the Fensterer case . In the Fensterer case, this Court was con- fro n ted with a situation in which an expert c ouldn ' t remember the basis for which three hairs had been forcibly pulled - - his conclusion that three hairs had been forcibly pulled from the victim's head. And this Court held that there was no violation of the confrontation clause, hut I think as Justice BlackMun pointed out in his concurring opinion in Pennsylvania versus !U.tcfe, the essence of Fensterer was that in failing to remember the expert undermined his credibility, and that was the key to the fensterer case, The credibility assessment could be made .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This witness could just as easily say, yes, I identified him, and told a lie, and then everything would be all right.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: But he didn't. It seems to me this case is different from Fensterer, and the reason it is different from Fensterer is bility, just as the Fifth Amendment assertion - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but your distinction, if I 1mCler- stand you, with Fensterer, is that that testimony is more -- it is easier to admit that because it is less credible.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: No, it is easier to admit that because the basis for judginq credibility has been established . It isn ' t -- the purpose of the confrontation --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the basis for questioning the reliability has been established, namely, he didn ' t remember the basis for the consluion. That is exactly what you have here.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: No, I respectfully disaqree . We have a very different situation here. In Fensterer, the lack of rnemory went to the expert ' s ability as an expert. In this case the lack of memory - -

turns.text: It is the ability of the witness to testify to the truth of ''he ultimate proposition. In both cases they are unable to do so hecause they can't explain the basis for their conclusion.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: But in the one, Fensterer, a basis is created through the questioning and throuqh the admission of introduced. suffered a memory loss has no relationship whatsoever to whether he ever observed his assailant. That is the credibility question in this case . The credibility question in Fensterer is, is this expert truly an expert if he cannot recall the basis for his testimony?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that is only part of it. The ultimate question is, is his opinion one we should acceot?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: And in Fensterer the trier of fact was given an opportunity to

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And one of the arguments for not accepting him is, he is not a good expert if he doesn ' t keep records and keep track of why he reaches his conclusion. He is not a very good witness for that proposition . You can make the same argument --

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: But you can ' t say that about a man who has suffered a memory loss . You can't say -- the point of view of lawyers and judqes and justices, yes , we point of view of jurors I don ' t think it is looked at in that The jurors see a very SYll\pathetic witness get on the stand and say one day it was vivid , I v ivid ly remember saying that this man did it to me, you know, I was injured, and maybe even by this man, this may be the man who cHd it, and now that kind of weight the jury is qoing to put on that under circumstances where

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are certainly free to make all the argume nts . to the jury about the unreliability of that kind of evidence as to, you know -- all the arquments you are making to us you could also make to ' the jury, and they are not unable to understand the nuances of th is sort of thing.

turns.name: IDP.S
turns.text: But it seems to me the courts have a threshold responsibility to determine whether evidence has been subjected to effective cross examination, and I aqree with Justice Blackmun that in the typical case, a simple case, simple questioninq will resolve that question, but in some cases , unless the confrontation clause is just a formality once you put the witness on the stand, in some cases we have yes, I did say that, but I don 't remember now whether I saw him or not, would your case be any different?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: The case would be different, and I think it would be analyzed differently. QUF:STION: Wny would it be different?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: All riqht, it would be analyzed differently. First, there's two parts to the confrontation clause . One is entitlement to confrontation, to cross examination, and I think in both you have just described and in this case, there was not an opportunity for effective cross examination . Then we go to the next step. Despite this lack of an opportunity for effective cross examination , are there indicia of reliability that indicate that this evidence ought to be admitted anyway? In your hvPothetical, there may be indicia of reliability that would permit it. In this Appeals ' ruling that this was unreliable under the factors articulated in Dutton versus Evans and Ohio versus Roberts. So I think the hypotheticals are different although they would be analyzed under the same structure .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could this have been cured hy i n struction?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: No, I don't believe it could be cured by instruction .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: Because, again, it is the type of evidence that the Court must make an initial assessment whether this evidence is the kind of evidence that the jury can now at this point assess for its reliability. That is a threshold question for the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It should have been excluded?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: It should have been excluded.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I thought your arqument was that he couldn't cross examine on it .

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: could not cross examine on it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if it is excl•1ded ym: couldn't cross examinP . wouldn't be here today. If it were excluded, it was the admits that. This critical evidence, Owens was my assailant , and then we have this black box, why, and the box is locked, and it is just as effectively locked with a memory loss as it is with the Fifth Amendment or as it is with a trial court ruling that you can ' t ask that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) identification?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: The only point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What have we qot to do with the box?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: The box . I am sorry , Your Honor. I am using an analogy. I am essentially saying the information tha , is crucial to this case, namely, the basis for the I tion, is not accessible to the trier of fact in the same way that it•wouldn't be accessible if Foster had taken the Fifth Amendment or if the t r ial court had said you can't ask questions on those g ro unds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr . Ides, let me qet back to the bank teller again. You say it makes the difference if the bank teller just says, I don ' t remember anything about . it. I remember is that I saw him. That would be enouqh to qet that out of the rule that you are urqinq on us.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: It may be enouqh. I think I would want more facts. I am certain that in your bank teller a case by case, we really can't a case by case analysis, just not for 600 district judges in the federal system that are coriducting tria l s, but for thousands and thousands of state court judqes that are con- ducting trials .

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: I have two responses to that, and I will go backwards. One is, we can avoid making this a rule f.or thousands and thousands of state court judges by focusinq on (1) (C) and concluding that this evidence should not have been admitted under that rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) for 600 federal judqes.

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: So we have narrowed it down to 600 federal judges, and I think federal judges are comoetent to assess this kind of a question. Aqain, the court ooinion, it seemed to me, would say this is the extraordinary case we referred to in Ohio versus Roberts. It is California- Green revisited in a sense, and I read California versus Green a little differently than the qovernment. It seems to me the question wasn't reserved in California versus Green. • The Court said there was a constitutional issue sufficient for us to answer it. The facts in this record are sufficien t . It was remanded to the California Supreme court and they concluded that the evidence in California versus

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask one other question, following up on Justice Scalia's thought? If one of the othe r wlittne sses, not the victim himself, has testified that they were facing one another when the assault occurred, then would you agree that his testimony would he admissible? MR . IDES: Again, I would -- that is very similar to Justice White ' s hypothetical. I think then we would have to assess it under the second component of confrontation c_}-ause -'\nalysis, whether there are sufficient indicia of reliability of this out of court statement, and I think we would have to look at that, but the same conclusion would be arrived at with respect to was there effective cross examination, and the answer, it seems to me, has to he no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if you say the other witness meets the indicia of reliability, no bias, and so forth, I am not victim ' s testimony would still be inadmissible?

turns.name: L\LLAN IDES
turns.text: No. the two components of the effective cross examination -- that is the Douglas, evidence still admissible because independen tly we say that it is reliable as judqes , and I am saying under the hyootheti- cal you have suqqested we would get to the question of whether it is desplte the lack of cross examination , are there adequate indicia of reliability , and in this case the Nin th Circuit said there weren ' t .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don ' t understand that . I just don't understand what you have said. If another witness -- you were in some doubt when the witness who forqets the whole thing says, I really can ' t say whether I saw it. I askeo you , suppose that witness remembers only that I saw the individual, I don ' t remember how, I don ' t remember what the liqht was, I ·don' t remember anything else, I saw him. You are not sure whether that would satisfy the rule, riqht? had an opportunity for effective cross examination. That is the part that we have been talking mostly now, whether the memory loss undermined that ability .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you say there never is when there is a memory loss. r think there are circumstances when the memory loss may bear the memory loss, if it is both critical and complete and independent of credibility, actually does undermine cross examination . Then we get to the second question. evidence still may be admissible if it is otherwise reliable, or because in this particular case based on your facts there are sufficient indicia of reliability, and that is Ohio versus Roberts . It seems to me that this r.ourt -- this case is a very straightforward application of that, and we have focused on whether there was an opportunity to effectively cross examine. I think from a practical lawyer ' s point of view, whether you are talking about a prosecutor or a defense attorney, if you took this case aside, they would say, you know-, I really couldn ' t effectively cross examine someone who had so complete a memory loss as to the most critical evidence in the case, and the confrontation clause is supposed to be a practical rule for advancing the inteqrity of the trial process, and r tnink we have to look at it like that . I would like to finally answer the second part of my answer to your question, Justice Rehnquist, on a narrow I think we have to recoqnize that there is a certain illoqic between saying a memory loss will not constitute a confron - tation clause violation, whereas an assertion of the Fifth Amendment would, even though the same evidence is being excluded from the trier .of fact, the same opportunity to cross examine is being eliminated in both cases. I think we have to recognize that. So then it seems to me the court's ruling should be modeled on cases such as Douqlas versus Alabama, Davis versus Alaska, Smith versus Illinois, in which the Court found that some trial court imposed restriction undermined defendant ' s ability to cross examine. So, first we would have to.make that finding . Second, I think we have to talk about the fact that it is a critical part of evidence, as this Court recoqnized as impo rtant in Dutton versus Evans. And third, the fact that in this case we have a complete memory loss , a complP.te memory loss, .and it's medica:tly . certitiable, and just like credibility of the statement, I saw Owens do it. The fact that he has a memory loss doesn't undermine in any way that he may well have seen owens do it . It doesn ' t add anything to it. It remains untouchable. of argument that was followed hy defense counsel in this case that was the most effective form of cross and exploitation of that cross examination , which was the suggestion that because there had been a number of visits of other people to Foster before Foster made his identificatio of Owens, perhaps Owens ' s name had been to him by these other people. That is precisely the kind of effective cross examination and exploitation of cross examination which was made possible in this case by the very fact that Foster was unable to say that I identified Owens as my assailant. He was unable to remember the reason for his identification, andi that opened up the arena for this precise l<:ind of argument that Mr . Ides has Pointed ·out that was made at trial, and it was made effectively by counsel . there is a further question which

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is pretty effective itself, you are saying, to be able to point out that he noesn ' t remember the basis for his identification.

turns.name: WILLIAM C. BRYSON
turns.text: Therefore it may have come from examination has to be effective, that opened up an avenue of effective cross examination for the defense. Now, the focus has also been put on this whole observation, whether for 602 reasons or for cross examination reasons. I would like to point out just two factual matters quickly. First of all, with re spect to the pipe, he made two comments about the pipe . One was the sound of the pipe that Mr. Ides referred to . He also said at Page 27 of the appendix, thinking back, it did have to be a pipe , that is about the right size, which suggests that he actually saw the pipe . Second, there were two witnesses, three, actually, Foster, one of whom was the person - - this was witness Bowers, who was hovering over Foster after the beating. There was another witness, however, Jeffery , who testified that
